notes: Self-correction within one turn: the user asks opencode to create a file containing a deliberate bug, read it back, notice the problem, and fix it — so the single turn carries MULTIPLE tool calls (write → read → edit/write) before it settles. The user-observable signal is that state stays continuously working across all the retry cycles — no flicker to ready or waiting between the buggy write and the corrective edit — and the turn finally settles to ready. This locks down the classifier's terminal-stop_reason gate: intermediate tool parts do NOT close the turn. In headless default (`opencode run`) tools auto-execute inside opencode's sandbox with NO interactive permission prompt, so the canonical arc is ready→working→ready and state NEVER enters waiting. Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), observed by the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source as the UUID-keyed ses_… session. Each tool call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (pending→running→completed/error) that the parser maps to function_call / function_call_output (parser.go parseToolPart); a tool-calls step-finish reason keeps the session working, and only step-finish reason=stop → turn_done settles working→ready (parser.go parseStepFinish) — so the write→read→edit sequence is one contiguous working span. The phase DSL has no tool-event form, so the iteration is asserted via the single uninterrupted working span it sustains plus the no-waiting invariant, not a dedicated phase — the ≥2 tool parts are verified in the SQLite store out-of-band at record time. session_birth is anchored to recording start and left UNPINNED so a transient proc-<PID> presession cannot steal identity and cascade same_session_as failures; turn_start (matching working) establishes the ses_… session identity that turn_done chains off via same_session_as. Same proven shape as the sibling opencode auto-executed-tool-call and turn-end-terminal-text specs; opencode's driver runs a blocking `opencode run` per turn (not a held-open REPL) and the whole multi-tool turn rides one `opencode run` subprocess, so the cell asserts the arc and omits a separate teardown/process_exited phase like its opencode siblings.

text: Session appears in ready within 2s of agent launch
PID bound to the ses_… session
User prompt requiring create-bug-then-read-back-and-fix drives the ses_… session ready → working

The write → read → corrective-edit tool calls auto-execute (no approval gate) inside one continuous working span — no flicker to ready or waiting between attempts — and the turn completes → back to ready on the same ses_… session; state never entered waiting
